papers.title: The Suicide Region: Option Games and the Race to Artificial General Intelligence
papers.abstract: Standard real options theory predicts delay in exercising the option to invest or deploy when extreme asset volatility or technological uncertainty are present. However, in the current race to develop artificial general intelligence (AGI), sovereign actors are exhibiting behaviors contrary to theoretical predictions: the US and China are accelerating AI investment despite acknowledging the potential for catastrophic failure from AGI misalignment. We resolve this puzzle by formalizing the AGI race as a continuous-time preemption game with endogenous existential risk. In our model, the cost of failure is no longer bounded only by the sunk cost of investment (I), but rather a systemic ruin parameter (D) that is correlated with development velocity and shared globally. As the disutility of catastrophe is embedded in both players' payoffs, the risk term mathematically cancels out of the equilibrium indifference condition. This creates a "suicide region" in the investment space where competitive pressures force rational agents to deploy AGI systems early, despite a negative risk-adjusted net present value. Furthermore, we show that "warning shots" (sub-existential disasters) will fail to deter AGI acceleration, as the winner-takes-all nature of the race remains intact. The race can only be halted if the cost of ruin is internalized, making safety research a prerequisite for economic viability. We derive the critical private liability threshold required to restore the option value of waiting and propose mechanism design interventions that can better ensure safe AGI research and socially responsible deployment.
papers.summary: This paper examines why the US and China are accelerating AGI development despite catastrophic risks, contradicting standard real options theory that predicts investment delay under high uncertainty. The authors develop a continuous-time preemption game model where both players face shared existential risk (parameter D) that scales with development speed, causing the risk terms to cancel out in equilibrium conditions. The key finding is that competitive dynamics create a "suicide region" where rational actors deploy AGI early despite negative risk-adjusted returns, and this race can only be stopped by internalizing ruin costs through sufficient private liability mechanisms.

papers.title: Existence of Equilibrium Mechanisms in Generalized Principal-Agent Problems with Interacting Teams
papers.abstract: We study incentive design when multiple principals simultaneously design mechanisms for their respective teams in environments with strategic spillovers. In this environment, each principal's set of incentive-compatible mechanisms--those that satisfy their own agents' incentive compatibility constraints--depends on the mechanisms offered by the other teams. Following a classic example by Myerson (1982), such games may lack equilibrium due to discontinuities in the correspondence of incentive-compatible mechanisms. We establish general conditions for equilibrium existence by introducing a novel approach that involves tracking both the outcome distributions along the truthful-obedient path and the sets of outcome distributions achievable through unilateral deviations, thereby providing a foundation for analyzing a wide range of multi-principal mechanism design with team production and agency problems.
papers.summary: This paper addresses the equilibrium existence problem in multi-principal mechanism design games where teams with internal agency problems interact through strategic spillovers. The authors develop a theoretical framework that tracks both truthful-obedient outcome distributions and deviations to resolve discontinuities in incentive-compatible mechanism correspondences that can prevent equilibrium existence (as in Myerson 1982). The key contribution is establishing general sufficient conditions for equilibrium existence in these complex environments by providing a novel approach to handle the interdependence between principals' mechanism choices when each faces internal team incentive compatibility constraints.

papers.title: Dynamic Mechanism Design without Monetary Transfers: A Queueing Theory Approach
papers.abstract: We study the design of optimal allocation mechanisms in an environment where agents and goods arrive stochastically. Agents have private types that determine the principal payoff. Either agents or goods can be held in a queue at a flow cost until allocation. The principal cannot use monetary transfers, but can verify agents types at a cost. We characterize the optimal mechanism at the steady state of the system. It is a dynamic threshold mechanism in which the principal sets type thresholds for agent admission and goods allocation. These thresholds depend on the current state of the mechanism. The model applies to public programs such as public housing and grant allocation, and to allocation problems within organizations such as capital budgeting.
papers.summary: This paper examines optimal allocation mechanism design when agents and goods arrive stochastically over time, agents have private information about their types, and monetary transfers are unavailable but costly type verification is possible. Using queueing theory, the authors theoretically characterize the steady-state optimal mechanism as a dynamic threshold policy where admission and allocation thresholds depend on current queue states (numbers of agents and goods waiting). The key contribution is showing that state-dependent thresholds optimally balance the trade-offs between waiting costs, verification costs, and allocation efficiency in dynamic environments without monetary instruments, with applications to public housing, grants, and organizational capital budgeting.

papers.title: A Real-Options-Aware Multi-Criteria Framework for Ex-Ante Real Estate Redevelopment Use Selection
papers.abstract: A growing share of the existing real estate stock exhibits persistent underperformance that can no longer be explained by cyclical market phases or inadequate maintenance alone. In many cases, technically recoverable assets located in non-marginal contexts fail to generate economic value consistent with the capital immobilized. This condition reflects a structural misalignment between intended use and effective demand rather than episodic market weakness, and calls for a decision framework capable of integrating value, risk, complexity, and irreversibility in strategic use selection. This study proposes a decision-analytic framework for the ex-ante selection of intended use in real estate redevelopment processes. The framework integrates real-options logic on irreversibility and managerial flexibility with a multi-criteria decision-analysis structure, enabling comparative evaluation of expected economic value, market and operational risk, technical and managerial complexity, and time-to-income. By treating redevelopment primarily as a problem of strategic option selection rather than design or financial optimization, the framework operationalizes option value preservation through disciplined ex-ante screening. Illustrative cases demonstrate how this integration of real options reasoning and MCDA reduces over-complexification and misalignment across different asset types and urban contexts.
papers.summary: This paper addresses the problem of selecting optimal land uses for underperforming real estate redevelopment by developing a decision framework that accounts for irreversibility and uncertainty. The methodology combines real options theory with multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) to evaluate alternative uses across dimensions of economic value, risk, complexity, and timing while preserving managerial flexibility. The key contribution is demonstrating how integrating real options logic with structured multi-criteria evaluation can reduce strategic misalignment and over-complexification in redevelopment decisions through disciplined ex-ante screening.

papers.title: Delegation in Strategic Environments and Equilibrium Uniqueness
papers.abstract: We ask when a normal-form game yields a single equilibrium prediction, even if players can coordinate by delegating play to an intermediary such as a platform or a cartel. Delegation outcomes are modeled via coarse correlated equilibria (CCE) when the intermediary cannot punish deviators, and via the set of individually rational correlated profiles (IRCP) when it can. We characterize games in which the IRCP or the CCE is unique, uncovering a structural link between these solution concepts. Our analysis also provides new conditions for the uniqueness of classical correlated and Nash equilibria that do not rely on the existence of dominant strategies. The resulting equilibria are robust to players' information about the environment, payoff perturbations, pre-play communication, equilibrium selection, and learning dynamics. We apply these results to collusion-proof mechanism design.
papers.summary: This paper investigates when normal-form games have unique equilibrium predictions when players can delegate decisions to intermediaries with varying enforcement capabilities. The methodology is purely theoretical, modeling delegation outcomes through coarse correlated equilibria (CCE) for intermediaries without punishment power and individually rational correlated profiles (IRCP) for those with punishment capabilities. The key contribution is characterizing games with unique CCE or IRCP solutions and establishing structural connections between these concepts, while providing new uniqueness conditions for classical equilibria that don't require dominant strategies, with applications to collusion-proof mechanism design.

papers.title: Revenue Non-monotonicity in Matching Markets
papers.abstract: The Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) mechanism is infamously revenue   non-monotone in combinatorial auctions. I.e., when a buyer increases   their value for a bundle of items, the total auction revenue may decrease.   Combinatorial auctions exhibit complementarities which broadly   result in complexities in auction theory. This brief note shows   that non-monotonicity in multi-item auctions is not a result of   complementarities, and in fact, VCG is revenue non-monotone even in matching   markets.
papers.summary: This paper investigates whether revenue non-monotonicity in VCG mechanisms stems from complementarities in multi-item settings. Using theoretical analysis of matching markets (which exhibit no complementarities), the author demonstrates that VCG mechanisms remain revenue non-monotone even when complementarities are absent. The key contribution is showing that revenue non-monotonicity is a fundamental property of VCG mechanisms that persists beyond combinatorial auctions with complementarities.

papers.title: Robust Mechanism Design with Anonymous Information
papers.abstract: In practice, auction data are often endogenously censored and anonymous, revealing only limited outcome statistics rather than full bid profiles. We study robust auction design when the seller observes only aggregated, anonymous order statistics and seeks to maximize worst-case expected revenue over all product distributions consistent with the observed statistic. We show that simple and widely used mechanisms are robustly optimal. Specifically, posted pricing is robustly optimal given the distribution of the highest value; the Myerson auction designed for the unique consistent i.i.d. distribution is robustly optimal given the lowest value distribution; and the second-price auction with an optimal reserve is robustly optimal when an intermediate order statistic is observed and the implied i.i.d. distribution is regular above its reserve. More generally, for a broad class of monotone symmetric mechanisms depending only on the top k order statistics, including multi-unit and position auctions, the worst-case revenue is attained under the i.i.d. distribution consistent with the observed k-th order statistic. Our results provide a tractable foundation for non-discriminatory auction design, where fairness and privacy are intrinsic consequences of the information structure rather than imposed constraints.
papers.summary: This paper examines optimal auction design when sellers only observe anonymous, aggregated statistics (like highest or lowest bids) rather than complete bidder information, asking how to maximize worst-case expected revenue across all possible underlying bidder distributions consistent with these limited observations. Using theoretical mechanism design with robust optimization, the authors prove that simple, widely-used mechanisms are optimal: posted prices when observing the highest value distribution, Myerson auctions for the lowest value distribution, and second-price auctions with reserves for intermediate order statistics. The key contribution demonstrates that standard auction formats are robustly optimal under realistic information constraints, providing theoretical justification for their widespread practical use while naturally achieving fairness and privacy without imposing additional constraints.

papers.title: The Architecture of Illusion: Network Opacity and Strategic Escalation
papers.abstract: Standard models of bounded rationality typically assume agents either possess accurate knowledge of the population's reasoning abilities (Cognitive Hierarchy) or hold dogmatic, degenerate beliefs (Level-$k$). We introduce the ``Connected Minds'' model, which unifies these frameworks by integrating iterative reasoning with a parameterized network bias. We posit that agents do not observe the global population; rather, they observe a sample biased by their network position, governed by a locality parameter $p$ representing algorithmic ranking, social homophily, or information disclosure. We show that this parameter acts as a continuous bridge: the model collapses to the myopic Level-$k$ recursion as networks become opaque ($p \to 0$) and recovers the standard Cognitive Hierarchy model under full transparency ($p=1$). Theoretically, we establish that network opacity induces a \emph{Sophisticated Bias}, causing agents to systematically overestimate the cognitive depth of their opponents while preserving the log-concavity of belief distributions. This makes $p$ an actionable lever: a planner or platform can tune transparency, globally or by segment (a personalized $p_k$), to shape equilibrium behavior. From a mechanism design perspective, we derive the \emph{Escalation Principle}: in games of strategic complements, restricting information can maximize aggregate effort by trapping agents in echo chambers where they compete against hallucinated, high-sophistication peers. Conversely, we identify a \emph{Transparency Reversal} for coordination games, where maximizing network visibility is required to minimize variance and stabilize outcomes. Our results suggest that network topology functions as a cognitive zoom lens, determining whether agents behave as local imitators or global optimizers.
papers.summary: This paper investigates how network structure and information opacity affect strategic reasoning in games by introducing the "Connected Minds" model that bridges Level-k and Cognitive Hierarchy frameworks. Using theoretical analysis, the authors show that a locality parameter p governs how agents sample information about opponents' reasoning abilities, with network opacity (low p) causing agents to systematically overestimate opponents' cognitive sophistication due to "Sophisticated Bias." The key contribution is the "Escalation Principle": in games with strategic complements, platforms can maximize aggregate effort by restricting information transparency, trapping agents in echo chambers where they compete against perceived high-sophistication opponents.

papers.title: Filling Positions Without Transfers: Screening on Outside Options
papers.abstract: A designer offers vertically-differentiated positions to agents in the absence of transfers. Agents have private outside options and may reject their offers ex-post. The designer has preferences over the quantity of agents who accept each position. We show that under a general condition on the distribution of outside options, an optimal mechanism for the designer offers all agents an identical lottery, and we characterize this mechanism. When our condition does not hold, the optimal mechanism may require screening agents by offering a menu of distinct lotteries. Our results follow from a decomposition of agents' participation probabilities in any feasible mechanism.
papers.summary: This paper examines how a designer should optimally allocate vertically-differentiated positions to agents when transfers are not allowed and agents have private information about their outside options that determine their ex-post participation. Using mechanism design theory, the authors decompose participation probabilities across feasible mechanisms and establish conditions under which the optimal mechanism involves either a single lottery offered to all agents or a screening menu of distinct lotteries based on agents' outside option distributions. The key contribution is showing that under a general distributional condition, offering identical lotteries to all agents is optimal, but when this condition fails, screening becomes necessary to maximize the designer's objective over position-specific acceptance rates.

papers.title: Lies, Labels, and Mechanisms
papers.abstract: We test whether lying aversion can steer equilibrium selection in mechanism design. In a principal-worker environment, the direct mechanism admits two dominant-strategy equilibria: the designer's target and a worker-optimal outcome. We show this limitation persists for all robust mechanisms, then ask whether framing misreports as explicit lies helps. We develop a 2X2 experiment that varies direct vs. extended mechanisms with implicit vs. explicit messages. We find that framing misreporting of type as an explicit lie shifts play away from the worker-optimal outcome toward truthful reporting, raising designer payoffs with minimal efficiency loss. These findings indicate that lying aversion is an effective lever for aligning behavior with social objectives.
papers.summary: This paper investigates whether lying aversion can resolve equilibrium selection problems in mechanism design when multiple dominant-strategy equilibria exist. Using a 2×2 laboratory experiment, the authors test direct versus extended mechanisms with implicit versus explicit message framing in a principal-worker setting where workers can either report truthfully (designer-optimal) or misreport (worker-optimal). The key finding is that explicitly framing misreports as "lies" significantly increases truthful reporting and designer payoffs compared to implicit messaging, demonstrating that lying aversion can effectively guide equilibrium selection toward socially preferred outcomes.

papers.title: Pricing Discrete and Nonlinear Markets With Semidefinite Relaxations
papers.abstract: Nonconvexities in markets with discrete decisions and nonlinear constraints make efficient pricing challenging, often necessitating subsidies. A prime example is the unit commitment (UC) problem in electricity markets, where costly subsidies are commonly required. We propose a new pricing scheme for nonconvex markets with both discreteness and nonlinearity, by convexifying nonconvex structures through a semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxation and deriving prices from the relaxation's dual variables. When the choice set is bounded, we establish strong duality for the SDP, which allows us to extend the envelope theorem to the value function of the relaxation. This extension yields a marginal price signal for demand, which we use as our pricing mechanism. We demonstrate that under certain conditions-for instance, when the relaxation's right hand sides are linear in demand-the resulting lost opportunity cost is bounded by the relaxation's optimality gap. This result highlights the importance of achieving tight relaxations. The proposed framework applies to nonconvex electricity market problems, including for both direct current and alternating current UC. Our numerical experiments indicate that the SDP relaxations are often tight, reinforcing the effectiveness of the proposed pricing scheme. Across a suite of IEEE benchmark instances, the lost opportunity cost under our pricing scheme is, on average, 46% lower than that of the commonly used fixed-binary pricing scheme.
papers.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of efficient pricing in nonconvex markets with discrete decisions and nonlinear constraints, using electricity unit commitment as the primary application. The methodology employs semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxations to convexify the nonconvex market structure, with prices derived from the relaxation's dual variables and theoretical analysis establishing strong duality and extending the envelope theorem to bound lost opportunity costs by the relaxation's optimality gap. The key contribution is a new pricing scheme that reduces lost opportunity costs by an average of 46% compared to fixed-binary pricing across IEEE benchmark instances, with theoretical guarantees linking pricing performance to relaxation tightness.

papers.title: JSAM: Privacy Straggler-Resilient Joint Client Selection and Incentive Mechanism Design in Differentially Private Federated Learning
papers.abstract: Differentially private federated learning faces a fundamental tension: privacy protection mechanisms that safeguard client data simultaneously create quantifiable privacy costs that discourage participation, undermining the collaborative training process. Existing incentive mechanisms rely on unbiased client selection, forcing servers to compensate even the most privacy-sensitive clients ("privacy stragglers"), leading to systemic inefficiency and suboptimal resource allocation. We introduce JSAM (Joint client Selection and privacy compensAtion Mechanism), a Bayesian-optimal framework that simultaneously optimizes client selection probabilities and privacy compensation to maximize training effectiveness under budget constraints. Our approach transforms a complex 2N-dimensional optimization problem into an efficient three-dimensional formulation through novel theoretical characterization of optimal selection strategies. We prove that servers should preferentially select privacy-tolerant clients while excluding high-sensitivity participants, and uncover the counter-intuitive insight that clients with minimal privacy sensitivity may incur the highest cumulative costs due to frequent participation. Extensive evaluations on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate that JSAM achieves up to 15% improvement in test accuracy compared to existing unbiased selection mechanisms while maintaining cost efficiency across varying data heterogeneity levels.
papers.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of designing efficient client selection and compensation mechanisms in differentially private federated learning, where privacy protection costs discourage client participation. The authors develop JSAM, a Bayesian-optimal theoretical framework that jointly optimizes client selection probabilities and privacy compensation, transforming a 2N-dimensional optimization into a tractable three-dimensional problem. The key contribution is proving that servers should preferentially select privacy-tolerant clients over "privacy stragglers," achieving up to 15% accuracy improvements over unbiased selection mechanisms while maintaining budget efficiency.

papers.title: Signaling in Data Markets via Free Samples
papers.abstract: We study a setting in which a data buyer seeks to estimate an unknown parameter by purchasing samples from one of K data sellers. Each seller has privately known data quality (e.g., high vs. low variance) and a private per-sample cost. We consider a multi-stage game in which the first stage is a free-trial stage in which the sellers have the option of signaling data quality by offering a few samples of data for free. Buyers update their beliefs based on the sample variance of the free data and then run a procurement auction to buy data in a second stage. For the auction stage, we characterize an approximately optimal Bayesian incentive compatible mechanism: the buyer selects a single seller by minimizing a belief-adjusted virtual cost and chooses the purchased sample size as a function of posterior quality and virtual cost. For the free-trial stage, we characterize the equilibrium, taking the above mechanism as the continuation game. Free trials may fail to emerge: for some parameters, all sellers reveal zero samples. However, under sufficiently strong competition (large K), there is an equilibrium in which sellers reveal the maximum allowable number of samples; in fact, it is the unique equilibrium.
papers.summary: This paper examines how data sellers can signal their quality through free samples when competing to sell data to buyers who need to estimate unknown parameters. The authors develop a theoretical multi-stage game model where sellers first optionally provide free samples to signal data quality, then buyers update beliefs and run a procurement auction. The key finding is that free trials may not emerge in equilibrium under weak competition, but with sufficiently strong competition (large K), sellers will provide the maximum number of free samples in a unique equilibrium, with the buyer optimally selecting sellers based on belief-adjusted virtual costs.

papers.title: Truthful Reverse Auctions for Adaptive Selection via Contextual Multi-Armed Bandits
papers.abstract: We study the problem of selecting large language models (LLMs) for user queries in settings where multiple LLM providers submit the cost of solving a query. From the users' perspective, choosing an optimal model is a sequential, query-dependent decision problem: high-capacity models offer more reliable outputs but are costlier, while lightweight models are faster and cheaper. We formalize this interaction as a reverse auction design problem with contextual online learning, where the user adaptively discovers which model performs best while eliciting costs from competing LLM providers. Existing multi-armed bandit (MAB) mechanisms focus on forward auctions and social welfare, leaving open the challenges of reverse auctions, provider-optimal outcomes, and contextual adaptation. We address these gaps by designing a resampling-based procedure that generalizes truthful forward MAB mechanisms to reverse auctions and prove that any monotone allocation rule with this procedure is truthful. Using this, we propose a contextual MAB algorithm that learns query-dependent model quality with sublinear regret. Our framework unifies mechanism design and adaptive learning, enabling efficient, truthful, and query-aware LLM selection.
papers.summary: This paper addresses how to design truthful reverse auctions for selecting large language models (LLMs) when providers submit costs and users must adaptively learn which models perform best for different query types. The methodology combines mechanism design theory with contextual multi-armed bandits, developing a resampling-based procedure that extends existing truthful forward auction mechanisms to reverse auctions while proving truthfulness under monotone allocation rules. The key contribution is a unified framework that achieves truthful, query-aware LLM selection with sublinear regret, bridging mechanism design and online learning for provider-optimal outcomes in contextual settings.
